1: Isaiah 43:1 pivots from judgment (Isaiah 42:24-25) to comfort with the 'but now' formula: 'veat'ah ko-amar YHWH bore'akha Ya'aqov veyotsrekha Yisrael, but now, thus says YHWH your creator, Jacob, and your former, Israel.' The double creative act: bore (created = the bara of Gen 1, the out-of-nothing creation) and yotser (formed = the yatsar of Genesis 2:7, the clay-molding of the human body) — both creation-verbs applied to Israel's national formation. The reversal of Isaiah 42:24-25's judgment: despite the burning, YHWH's creative relationship with Israel continues. 'Do not be afraid, for I have redeemed you' (al-tira ki ge'altikhah = do not fear, for I have redeemed you) — the go'el-action as completed past (ge'altikhah = perfect tense, I have already redeemed you) even before the exile ends. 'I have called you by name — you are mine' (qarati beshmekha li-attah = I called by your name, you are mine). The name-calling = intimate personal knowledge (Exodus 33:12: 'I know you by name'; John 10:3: 'the shepherd calls his own sheep by name') and the covenant declaration: 'you are mine' (li-attah = to me you belong).
2: 'When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; through the rivers, they will not overwhelm you. When you walk through the fire, you will not be scorched; the flame will not set you ablaze' — the four-element promise of divine presence in the midst of danger: 'ki-ta'avor bamayim itti-ani, when you pass through the waters, I will be with you.' The key word is 'through' (b- = in/through, not 'around'): the promise is not protection from the danger but presence within it. The waters and rivers = the Red Sea (the Exodus archetype, Exod 14) and possibly the Jordan (Josh 3). The fire and flame = possibly the furnace of Dan 3 (Shadrach, Meshach, Abednego), or the general image of severe trial by fire. All four elements (water, rivers, fire, flame) = the full spectrum of destructive natural forces as figures for the trials of exile and the journey home. 'They will not overwhelm you' (velo yishtefukhah = will not flood/overwhelm you; shataph = to overflow, Exodus 15:10: 'the sea covered them') and 'will not set you ablaze' (velo-tivaar bekha = will not burn on you). The promise is not immunity but accompaniment.
3: 'For I am the LORD your God, the Holy One of Israel, your Savior. I give Egypt as your ransom, Ethiopia and Seba in your place' — the theological ground of the promise: 'ki ani YHWH Elohekha qedosh Yisrael moshi'ekha, for I, YHWH your God, the Holy One of Israel, your Savior.' Three titles: YHWH (covenant name), qedosh Yisrael (Holy One of Israel = Isaiah's distinctive title, appearing 12x in Isaiah 40-55), moshi'ekha (your Savior = from yasha = to deliver/save; cf. Isaiah 43:11: 'there is no savior apart from me'). The ransom: 'natati khefer Mitsrayim Kush uSeva tachtekha, I give Egypt as your ransom, Cush and Seba in your place.' The theological interpretation of Persian history: Cyrus who liberated Israel (539 BCE) later conquered Egypt through his son Cambyses (525 BCE). The geopolitical price of Israel's liberation was Egypt's domination. The hefker (ransom = the payment that cancels a debt or redeems a captive; Exodus 21:30) is YHWH's term for the geopolitical exchange: Egypt exchanged for Israel.
4: 'Because you are precious in my eyes and honored, and because I love you, I will give people in exchange for you, nations in place of your life' — the divine love declaration: 'me'asher yaqart ve'einai nikhbadta va'ani ahavtikhah, because you are precious in my eyes and honored, and I love you.' The three terms of divine valuation: (1) yaqart (you are precious/costly = from yaqar = to be precious, rare, of great price; Psalms 72:14: 'precious is their blood in his sight'); (2) nikhbadta (you are honored = from kavad = to be weighty, glorious, honored — the kavod-root); (3) ahavtikhah (I love you = the explicit declaration of divine love). The divine 'I love you' addressed to the corporate Israel is rare in OT prophecy (Malachi 1:2: 'I have loved you says the LORD'; Jeremiah 31:3: 'I have loved you with an everlasting love'). The love as the motivation for the ransom: YHWH gave Egypt because he loves Israel. 'I will give people in exchange for you, nations in place of your life' (ve'eten adam tachtekhah ule'ummim tachat nafshekha = I give a man/people in your stead and nations in exchange for your life).
5: 'Do not be afraid, for I am with you. I will bring your offspring from the east and gather you from the west' — the ingathering from east and west: 'al-tira ki-imkha-ani, do not be afraid, for I am with you.' The fifth occurrence of the 'do not fear' formula in Isaiah 41-43, each one expanding the grounds for confidence. 'I will bring your offspring from the east' (mimmizrach avi zar'ekha, from the east I will bring your offspring/seed) and 'gather you from the west' (umimma'arav aqabbetstsekhah = from the west I will gather you). The ingathering of the Diaspora from multiple directions was partially fulfilled in the return under Zerubbabel (Ezra 1-2), Ezra (Ezra 7-10), and Nehemiah (Neh 1-7), and fully anticipated in the eschatological ingathering (Matthew 24:31: 'he will send his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds'; Revelation 7:9: 'a great multitude that no one could count, from every nation').
6: 'I will say to the north, Give them back! and to the south, Do not hold them back. Bring my sons from far away, my daughters from the ends of the earth' — the four-directional ingathering: 'omar latsafon teni ueladdrom al-tikh'ali, I will say to the north: give back! and to the south: do not withhold!' The north and south (adding to the east and west of v5) = all four directions = the complete dispersion of the Diaspora gathered from every compass point. 'Bring my sons from far away, my daughters from the ends of the earth' (havi'i vanai merachok uvnotai miqtseh ha'arets = bring my sons from far away and my daughters from the end of the earth) — the gender-inclusive ingathering: sons and daughters, not merely male warriors or adult males. The family-gathering language: 'my sons' (vanai = my sons, YHWH's children in the covenant-family sense of Exodus 4:22: 'Israel is my firstborn son') and 'my daughters' (uvnotai = and my daughters) = all of Israel as YHWH's children.
7: 'Everyone who bears my name, whom I created for my glory, whom I formed and made' — the identity-criterion for the ingathering: 'kol hanniqra vishmi, everyone who is called by my name.' The criterion is not ethnic purity or location but the divine name-bearing (cf. Numbers 6:27: 'so they will put my name on the Israelites, and I will bless them'; Deuteronomy 28:10: 'then all the peoples of the earth will see that you are called by the name of the LORD'). Three creation-verbs: (1) 'whom I created' (bera'tiiv = I created him, the bara of Gen 1); (2) 'formed' (yetsartiiv = I formed him, the yatsar of Genesis 2:7); (3) 'made' (va'assitiiv = and I made him, the asah of Genesis 1:26-27). The triple creation = the comprehensive divine investment in this people. The purpose: 'likevodi bera'tiiv = for my glory I created him' — existence for doxological purpose (cf. Isaiah 42:12: 'ascribe glory to the LORD').
8: 'Bring out the people who are blind — though they have eyes — and those who are deaf — though they have ears!' — the paradox of the blind witnesses: 'hotssi am-iver ve'einayim yesh vecheresh ve'oznayim lo, bring out the people who are blind, yet have eyes; and deaf, yet have ears.' The am-iver (blind people = the corporate servant-Israel of Isaiah 42:18-20 who could not see YHWH's acts) are now summoned as witnesses to the divine courtroom. The qualification 've'einayim yesh = yet having eyes' = they are not organically blind (no physical eyes), they are functionally blind (spiritual blindness despite physical sight). The same paradox applied to deafness. The irony: the witnesses called to testify to YHWH's uniqueness are people who have been unable to perceive YHWH's acts — yet their very existence (as a people who have been preserved despite exile) is itself the testimony. Their corporate survival is the evidence, even if they cannot articulate it.
9: 'Let all the nations gather together; let the peoples assemble. Who among them declared this, or foretold us what has happened before? Let them bring their witnesses to prove themselves right — or let them hear and say, It is true' — the divine courtroom (cf. Isaiah 41:1, 21-29) reconvened with both Israel and the nations: 'kol-haggoyim niqbetsu yachdav veyease'afu le'ummim, all the nations gathered together and the peoples assembled.' The comprehensive assembly: all nations (kol-haggoyim) and all peoples (le'ummim). The dual evidentiary challenge: (1) 'Who among them declared this, or foretold what has happened before?' (mi vahem yaggid zot verishonut yashmie'enu = who among them declares this and the former things announces to us) — the prediction test of Isaiah 41:22-23; (2) 'Let them bring their witnesses to prove themselves right' (yittenu eideihem veyitsdaqu = let them bring their witnesses so they may be declared right) — legal witnesses for the idols' case. 'Or let them hear and say, It is true' (veyishme'u veyo'meru emet = or let them hear and say: truth) — even the option of simply agreeing with YHWH is offered.
10: 'You are my witnesses, declares the LORD, along with my servant whom I have chosen — so that you may know and believe me and understand that I am the one. Before me no god was formed, and after me there will be none' — Israel's designation as YHWH's witnesses: 'attem edai ne'um-YHWH, you are my witnesses, declares YHWH.' The Israel brought as blind witnesses (v8) is now commissioned as YHWH's witnesses. Along with 'my servant whom I have chosen' (avdi asher bechharti = my servant whom I have chosen = the individual Servant of 42:1 who knows and sees, providing what the blind-Israel cannot). The purpose: 'lema'an tede'u veta'aminu li vehavinu ki-ani hu = so that you may know and believe/trust me and understand that I am he.' The three levels of knowledge: (1) yada (know = experiential knowledge); (2) aman (believe/trust = the same root as amen = reliable, settled confidence); (3) bin (understand = comprehensive insight). 'Before me no god was formed, and after me there will be none' (lifanai lo-notsar El ve'acharai lo yihyeh = before me no god was formed and after me there will not be) — the absolute monotheism expressed as temporal exclusivity.
11: 'I, I am the LORD, and apart from me there is no savior' — the emphatic monotheistic exclusivity: 'anoki anoki YHWH ve'en mibbal'adai moshi'a, I, I, YHWH, and there is no savior apart from me.' The doubled first-person pronoun (anoki anoki = I, I) = the highest possible emphasis in Hebrew (cf. v25: 'I, I am the one who blots out your transgressions'). The 'apart from me' (mibbal'adai = away from me/except through me) qualifier: not 'I am better than other saviors' but 'there is no other in the savior-category.' The en moshi'a (there is no savior) is an ontological claim, not merely a comparative boast. The Babylonian gods were identified as 'saviors' in their texts — Marduk saved Babylon from Tiamat, Bel protected the nation. YHWH's claim denies not their power but their existence as a category.
12: 'I declared, I saved, and I proclaimed — when there was no foreign god among you. You are my witnesses, declares the LORD, and I am God' — YHWH's historical track record as the ground of the witness-testimony: 'ani higgadti vehoshi'ati vehishma'ti, I declared and saved and proclaimed.' The three-stage testimony: (1) higgadti (I declared/announced = the prophetic prediction before the event); (2) hoshi'ati (I saved = the historical act of deliverance); (3) vehishma'ti (I proclaimed/made heard = the testimony after the event). 'When there was no foreign god among you' (ve'en bakhem zar = and there was no strange/foreign among you) = a reminder that during the Exodus and conquest, Israel was not contaminated by foreign religious systems — the purity of YHWH's relationship with early Israel as the baseline. 'You are my witnesses, declares the LORD, and I am God' (attem edai ne'um-YHWH va'ani El = you are my witnesses, declares YHWH, and I am God) — the commissioning formula repeated, now with the simple and absolute 'I am God' (va'ani El).
13: 'Even from eternity I am he. No one can deliver from my hand. When I act, who can reverse it?' — YHWH's eternal, irresistible sovereignty: 'af-meyom ani hu, even from the beginning of the day I am he.' The meyom (from the day = from the beginning of time/eternity = the 'day' as the start of created time) — YHWH's existence predating the created order. 'No one can deliver from my hand' (ve'en miyyadi matsil = and there is no one from my hand who delivers) — the absolute scope of divine sovereignty: no other power can override YHWH's acts or rescue those whom YHWH holds. 'When I act, who can reverse it?' (ef'al umi yeshivenah = I will act and who will turn it back?) — the irresistibility of divine purpose. The three claims (eternal being, inescapable power, irreversible action) form a complete sovereignty-statement that is simultaneously comfort (for Israel) and judgment (for Babylon).
14: 'This is what the LORD says — your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel: For your sake I am sending against Babylon and will bring down all of them as fugitives — even the Chaldeans — in the ships that were once their pride' — the specific Babylon-judgment oracle: 'ko-amar YHWH go'alekhem qedosh Yisrael, thus says YHWH your redeemer, the Holy One of Israel.' The twofold divine title (Go'el + Holy One of Israel) anchors the judgment in covenant-redemption. 'For your sake I am sending against Babylon' (lema'ankhem shillachti Babelah = for your sake I have sent to Babylon) — the motivation: Israel's redemption motivates the Babylon-judgment. 'And will bring down all of them as fugitives' (vehoradti varikhim kullam = and I will bring down all of them as fugitives/ones who flee). 'Even the Chaldeans, in the ships that were once their pride' (veKhasdim be'oniyot rinnatam = and the Chaldeans in the ships of their shouting/cry of joy). The Chaldeans (the ruling class of Babylon) who had fleets on the Euphrates and Persian Gulf — their ships of pride become ships of flight. The proud Babylonian maritime commerce reversed into a means of humiliating escape.
15: 'I am the LORD, your Holy One, Israel's Creator, your King' — the fourfold divine self-identification: 'ani YHWH qedoshshekhem bore Yisrael malkekkhem, I, YHWH, your Holy One, creator of Israel, your King.' The four titles: (1) YHWH (covenant name); (2) qedoshshekhem (your Holy One = the personal possession of the holy title); (3) bore Yisrael (creator of Israel = echoing 43:1 and connecting the creation-theology to the kingship); (4) malkekkhem (your King = the divine kingship over Israel, announced in Isaiah 33:22: 'YHWH is our king; it is he who will save us'). The divine King who created Israel is the same one who is about to defeat Babylon — the personal-national-cosmic scale of YHWH's lordship compressed into a four-word identity statement.
16: 'This is what the LORD says — he who makes a way through the sea and a path through the mighty waters' — the Exodus-credential introduction: 'ko-amar YHWH hannoten bayyam derekh, thus says YHWH who makes/gives in the sea a way.' The Exodus reference: the crossing of the Red Sea (Exodus 14:21-22: 'Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and all that night the LORD drove the sea back with a strong east wind... the waters were divided'). 'A path through the mighty waters' (uvemayim azzim netivah = and in the mighty waters a path/path-way). The natib (path = a narrow, rarely traveled way through difficult terrain) in the 'mighty waters' (mayim azzim = strong, powerful waters) = the passage through the Red Sea as a narrow path through impossible terrain. This credential introduces the oracle of v17 before YHWH claims something even greater (the new thing of v19) that supersedes even this Exodus miracle.
17: 'Who brought out chariot and horse, army and warrior together — they lay down and could not rise; they were extinguished, quenched like a wick' — the completion of the Exodus reference: 'hammotsi rekhev-wasussus chayil va'izuz yachdav, who brings out chariot and horse, army and warrior together.' The four military elements of Pharaoh's pursuing army: rekhev (chariot), sussus (horse), chayil (army/force), ve'izuz (and warrior/mighty one) — all of them together (yachdav). The result: 'yishkavu bal-yaqumu do'aku khappishtah kammu, they lay down and could not rise; they were extinguished like a wick.' The kammu (like a wick = the wick of the oil lamp, which is extinguished by pinching) recalls Isaiah 42:3's 'smoldering wick he will not snuff out' — the Servant does not extinguish the barely-burning wick of the vulnerable; but YHWH does extinguish Pharaoh's army 'like a wick.' The irony: the Egyptian army, which seemed as inexorable as the mighty waters, was extinguished as easily as a lamp-flame.
18: 'Do not remember the former things, or consider the events of long ago' — the startling command to forget the Exodus: 'al-tizkeru rishonut, do not remember the former things.' The rishonut (former things = the same term as Isaiah 42:9's 'former things have come to pass') = the Exodus, the wilderness, the conquest — the paradigmatic salvation events that Isaiah has been citing as credentials (Isaiah 41:17-20; Isaiah 42:14-16; Isaiah 43:16-17). 'Or consider the events of long ago' (vequdemiyot al-titevanenu = and the things of old, do not contemplate). This command is shocking given the entire theology of Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 5:15: 'remember that you were slaves in Egypt'; Isaiah 8:2: 'remember how the LORD your God led you through the wilderness') where the Exodus-memory is the primary theological ground for obedience. The command here is not to forget the Exodus entirely but to not allow past salvation to limit expectation of future salvation — the new thing is so much greater that comparing it to the Exodus is inadequate.
19: 'Look — I am doing something new; even now it springs up. Do you not see it? I am making a way in the wilderness and rivers in the desert' — the 'new thing' oracle: 'hineni oseh chadashah attah titsmach halo tede'uha = behold I am doing a new thing; now it springs up — do you not know it?' The chadashah (new thing = the feminine adjective for 'new,' the hadashot of Isaiah 42:9) springing up like new vegetation from the ground (titsmach = present tense: it is already sprouting; the new exodus has already begun in the divine plan even before it is visible). 'I am making a way in the wilderness and rivers in the desert' (asim bamidbar derekh bishimon neharot = I am setting in the wilderness a way, in the wasteland rivers). The wilderness way and desert rivers invert both the Exodus ('a way through the sea,' v16) and the wilderness experience (where there was no water without miracle) — the new Exodus will not need the Red Sea path; the desert itself becomes a highway with rivers alongside. The return from Babylon is the historical fulfillment; the eschatological return (Revelation 22:1-2: 'the river of the water of life') is the ultimate one.
20: 'The wild animals will honor me — the jackals and the ostriches — because I provide water in the wilderness and rivers in the desert, to give drink to my chosen people' — the non-human creation joining the praise: 'tekabbedbani chayat hassadeh tanniyim uvenot ya'anah, the creatures of the field will honor me — the jackals and the ostriches.' The tan (jackals = the howling scavengers of the desert and ruined cities; Isaiah 34:13; Isaiah 35:7; Jeremiah 9:11) and benot ya'anah (ostriches = literally 'daughters of the owl/howl,' the large flightless bird of the desert) = the most desolate desert creatures. Even they honor YHWH because the desert water benefits them: 'for I provide water in the wilderness and rivers in the desert' (ki natati vamidbar mayim neharot bishimon = for I give in the wilderness water, rivers in the wasteland). 'To give drink to my chosen people' (leshaqot ami bechiiri = to give drink to my people, my chosen one). The animals benefit collaterally from the grace extended to Israel — the restoration of Israel restores the whole creation (Romans 8:19-22: 'the creation waits in eager expectation for the children of God to be revealed').
21: 'The people I formed for myself, that they might declare my praise' — the purpose-statement of Israel's existence: 'am-zu yatsarti li tehillati yesapperu, this people I formed for myself — my praise they will tell.' The yatsarti (I formed = the potter's forming, Genesis 2:7; Isaiah 43:1; Isaiah 43:7) applied to Israel as YHWH's crafted work. 'For myself' (li = for/to me) — Israel's existence is not self-referential but YHWH-referential. 'My praise they will tell' (tehillati yesapperu = my praise they will declare) — the sappar (to declare, tell, recount) of YHWH's tehillah (praise/glory = his praiseworthiness). The purpose: Israel is the praise-declaring community. Exodus 19:6 ('a kingdom of priests and a holy nation'); 1 Peter 2:9 ('a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people belonging to God, that you may declare the praises of him who called you out of darkness into his wonderful light') applies this purpose-statement to the church. The mkt-context of this verse: the entire history of Israel — creation, exodus, exile, restoration — is teleologically oriented toward the praise of YHWH.
22: 'Yet, Jacob, you have not called on me; you have grown weary of me, O Israel' — the indictment pivoting from the restoration promise (vv18-21) to Israel's failure: 'velo oti qara'ta Ya'aqov ki-yag'ata bi Yisrael, and not me you have called on, Jacob; for you have grown weary of me, Israel.' The pivot from 'I formed you for my praise' (v21) to 'you have not called on me' = the gap between YHWH's purpose for Israel and Israel's actual practice. The 'not called on me' (lo qara'ta = did not call on me) = the failure of prayer and worship — YHWH as the partner who was not sought. 'You have grown weary of me' (ki yag'ata bi = for you have been wearied by me) — the irony: YHWH does not grow weary (Isaiah 40:28: 'he will not grow faint or weary') but Israel has grown weary of him. The spiritual exhaustion of a community that found YHWH's demands burdensome.
23: 'You have not brought me sheep for burnt offerings, and your sacrifices have not honored me. I have not burdened you with grain offerings or wearied you with incense' — the inversion of the typical sacrifice-complaint: not that Israel brought bad sacrifices but that they did not bring even the sheep, and YHWH had not required the heavy tribute they apparently imagined. 'Lo-heveeta li seh olotekhah, you did not bring me a sheep of your burnt offerings' — the most basic sacrifice not offered. 'Uvezevachekhah lo kibadtani, and with your sacrifices you did not honor me' — the honor (kabod = weight/glory) that sacrifice was designed to render was not rendered. But YHWH reverses the blame: 'lo he'avadtikha beminchah, I did not burden you with a grain offering.' The he'avadtikha (I did not make you serve/labor = I did not burden you as a slave-driver) — YHWH's requirements were not excessive. 'Nor wearied you with incense' (velo yega'tikhah billevonah = and I did not weary you with frankincense) — YHWH's actual requirements were light; Israel's complaint of burden was unjustified.
24: 'You have not bought me fragrant calamus with money, nor have you satisfied me with the fat of your sacrifices. But you have burdened me with your sins and wearied me with your iniquities' — the reversal of burden: 'lo-qanita li vekhessef qaneh, you did not buy for me with silver the calamus.' The qaneh (fragrant calamus/reed = the spice used in the anointing oil of Exodus 30:23 and the sacred incense blend) is expensive — it had to be purchased. Israel did not buy even this. 'Nor have you satisfied me with the fat of your sacrifices' (vechelev zevachekhah lo hirvitani = and the fat of your sacrifices you did not satisfy/drench me). The irony reversal: 'But you have burdened me with your sins' (akh he'avadtani bechattotekha = but you have made me labor/serve with your sins) and 'wearied me with your iniquities' (yega'tani ba'avonotekha = you have wearied me with your iniquities). The same vocabulary of burdening and wearying (avad and yaga) that YHWH refused to apply to his requirements is now attributed to Israel's sin — sin burdens and wearies even YHWH.
25: 'I, I am the one who blots out your transgressions for my own sake, and I will not remember your sins' — the grace counter-response: 'anoki anoki hu mocheh feshaekhah lema'ani, I, I am he who blots out your transgressions, for my own sake.' The emphatic anoki anoki (I, I = the same double pronoun as v11's 'I, I am the LORD') = maximum divine emphasis. The mocheh (blots out = to wipe a surface clean, Exodus 32:32-33: Moses asks YHWH to blot his name out of the book; Psalms 51:1,9: 'blot out my transgressions... blot out all my iniquity'). Crucially: 'lema'ani = for my own sake/for myself' — the forgiveness is self-motivated, grounded in YHWH's own character and will rather than in Israel's repentance or merit. The divine freedom to forgive purely out of sovereign grace. 'And I will not remember your sins' (vechatotekha lo ezkor = and your sins I will not remember) — the divine amnesia of forgiveness: not that YHWH loses information but that he chooses not to rehearse the sins in future judgment (Jeremiah 31:34: 'I will forgive their wickedness and will remember their sins no more').
26: 'Put me in remembrance; let us argue the matter together. Present your case, that you may be proved innocent' — the invitation to legal self-defense after the stunning grace of v25: 'hazkireni nishapetah yachdav, remind me, let us plead together.' The challenge: 'sapper attah lema'an titsddaq, you tell/recount so that you may be declared right.' YHWH invites Israel to present whatever case it can muster for its own innocence. The divine courtroom is open, the charges have been laid (vv22-24), and yet the invitation is genuinely offered — YHWH is not conducting a show trial but a real one. The irony: after v25's radical grace ('I blot out your transgressions for my own sake'), the invitation to 'be proved innocent' is somewhat ironic — Israel cannot prove its innocence (vv27-28 will confirm its guilt), but the offer of legal hearing is itself evidence of YHWH's commitment to fair process even in the act of grace.
27: 'Your first ancestor sinned, and your intermediaries have rebelled against me' — the verdict of the divine courtroom: 'avikha harishon chata umelitsaiykhah pash'a vi, your first father sinned and your interpreters/mediators have rebelled against me.' The 'first ancestor' (avikha harishon = your first father): most likely a reference to Jacob/Israel (the ancestor who gave the nation its name and who himself was a deceiver from birth, Genesis 25:26; Isaiah 27:36) rather than Adam, in context of the Jacob/Israel address of this chapter. The 'intermediaries' (melietsaiykhah = your interpreters/mediators = those who speak on behalf of Israel before YHWH: could be prophets, priests, or kings — all of whom 'rebelled'). The guilt is comprehensive: from the founding ancestor through all the institutional mediators — the entire covenant history is a story of rebellion. This explains why the forgiveness of v25 must be purely for YHWH's own sake — there is no innocence anywhere in Israel's leadership to appeal to.
28: 'So I allowed the sanctuary's leaders to be defiled, and I gave Jacob over to destruction and Israel to scorn' — the consequence of the foundational sin: 'va'achalel sarei qodesh, and I desecrated/defiled the leaders of the sanctuary.' The achalel (profaned/defiled = from chalal = to pierce, defile, make common; the same root as challelhu = the profaning of the Sabbath in Exodus 31:14) applied to the sarei qodesh (the leaders of the sanctuary = the priests and Levitical leaders). The desecration of the sanctuary leadership was the consequence of the ancestors' sin cascading through generations. 'And I gave Jacob over to destruction' (ve'etten lachereim Ya'aqov = and I gave Jacob to the ban/destruction). The cherem (the sacred ban/destruction = the same term as YHWH's ban on the nations in Isaiah 34:2; the military-religious term for total destruction applied now to Jacob). 'And Israel to scorn' (veYisrael ligduphim = and Israel to curses/reproaches) — the public humiliation of the Babylonian exile as YHWH's judicial act. The ending of Isaiah 43 without a final comfort note is intentional: the weight of inherited sin is the theological foundation for the grace-announcement that begins Isaiah 44.
